# **What emerging technologies are revolutionizing workplace safety management systems, and how can companies effectively integrate them into their operations? Include case studies from leading organizations and link to research articles on the impact of technology in safety.**

![What emerging technologies are revolutionizing workplace safety management systems, and how can companies effectively integrate them into their operations? Include case studies from leading organizations and link to research articles on the impact of technology in safety.](https://img.vorecol.com/ia-images/643/6353bc5ecc9d65fe73e43e156bfc09469256cc0c.jpg)

## 1. Harnessing AI for Enhanced Risk Assessment: Key Tools & Strategies for Employers

In an era where workplace safety is paramount, organizations are turning to artificial intelligence (AI) to revolutionize risk assessment processes. AI tools such as predictive analytics and machine learning algorithms have proven instrumental in identifying potential hazards before they escalate. For instance, a study from IBM highlights that businesses utilizing AI in risk management have seen a reduction in workplace incidents by up to 30% . By integrating real-time data from wearable technologies and IoT devices, employers can monitor employee movements and environmental conditions, thus pinpointing unsafe practices or unexpected hazards more accurately. Leading organizations like GE and Siemens have successfully adopted these strategies, demonstrating a proactive approach to safety that not only mitigates risks but also fosters a culture of well-being and trust among employees.

Case studies reveal the effectiveness of these AI solutions in practice. The construction giant, Skanska, implemented an AI-driven safety management system that utilizes historical data to predict and prevent accidents. The company reported a remarkable 50% decrease in accidents on their sites within just one year of implementation . Similarly, a research article published in the Journal of Safety Research found that companies leveraging AI for safety management experienced 40% fewer medical claims related to workplace injuries . These statistics underline the undeniable impact of technology in safety management, urging employers to harness AI not merely as a tool but as a vital component of their operational strategy.

![Vorecol, human resources management system](https://img.vorecol.com/barras_free/en/environment/barra-vorecol-work-environment-en-2.jpg)

## 2. IoT Solutions Transforming Safety Protocols: Case Studies from Top Corporations

The integration of Internet of Things (IoT) solutions in workplace safety protocols is reshaping how corporations manage risk and ensure employee safety. For instance, the global construction firm Skanska has implemented IoT-enabled wearables that monitor vital signs and environmental conditions in real-time, alerting workers and supervisors to hazards before accidents occur. This proactive approach not only enhances worker awareness but also reduces injury rates significantly, as evidenced by a report from the National Safety Council, which highlighted a 25% decrease in incidents for companies adopting such technologies .

Similarly, Siemens has employed smart sensors in their manufacturing facilities, providing actionable insights into equipment performance and detecting potential failures before they lead to dangerous incidents. A case study published in the Journal of Occupational Safety and Health demonstrates how Siemens's IoT deployment reduced machine-related injuries by 30% within the first year, showcasing the power of data-driven decision-making in enhancing safety protocols . Companies looking to integrate IoT technologies should consider starting with pilot programs to assess workflow impacts and employee feedback, using iterative improvements to scale solutions effectively. Implementing comprehensive training alongside technological advancements will further ensure that employees feel empowered and informed about new safety measures.

## 3. Virtual Reality Training: Revolutionizing Employee Safety Engagement

In today's fast-paced work environment, companies are increasingly turning to virtual reality (VR) training as a game-changer for enhancing employee safety engagement. By immersing employees in realistic safety scenarios, VR training reduces risk and boosts retention rates. A study by the American Society of Safety Professionals found that VR training can improve information retention by up to 75%, compared to traditional methods . For instance, Walmart has successfully implemented VR to train staff on emergency preparedness, resulting in a 10% reduction in injuries during high-stress situations. This not only enhances safety but also fosters a culture of proactive risk management within the organization.

The integration of VR training into workplace safety systems is not without its challenges, but pioneer organizations are leading the way. According to research from PwC, companies utilizing VR training report up to four times higher engagement among employees compared to conventional training methods. Boeing, for example, has embraced VR for its technicians, drastically cutting down training time and achieving a remarkable 30% increase in assembly accuracy, thereby minimizing potential safety hazards . These insights highlight how VR not only transforms the way businesses approach training but also reinforces a constant commitment to employee well-being, making safety an integral part of organizational culture.

## 4. Drones in Hazardous Work Environments: Successful Implementation Examples & Best Practices

Drones have emerged as a vital tool in hazardous work environments, offering both safety enhancements and operational efficiency. For instance, in the oil and gas industry, companies like BP have successfully implemented drone surveying to monitor pipeline integrity and inspect equipment at heights or in remote locations, minimizing worker exposure to dangerous conditions. A case study from the National Safety Council highlights that using drones can significantly reduce the time taken for inspections while ensuring that safety protocols are adhered to. This not only prolongs the operational lifespan of assets but also protects workers from potential accidents associated with manual inspections in hazardous zones ).

Best practices for using drones in such environments include proper training for operators and the integration of drone data into existing safety management systems. For instance, Chevron has adopted a comprehensive drone training program that emphasizes safety and compliance, which has led to fewer incidents on-site. Moreover, organizations are encouraged to complete risk assessments prior to employing drone technology, ensuring it aligns with safety regulations and workplace policies. A survey conducted by the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) indicates that companies that effectively leverage drone technology can enhance safety records while increasing efficiency ). By carefully incorporating drones into their workflows, businesses can transform their approach to safety management, allowing for proactive rather than reactive safety measures.

![Vorecol, human resources management system](https://img.vorecol.com/barras_free/en/environment/barra-vorecol-work-environment-en-4.jpg)

## 5. Wearable Technology: Real-Time Monitoring for Improved Safety Compliance

Wearable technology is transforming workplace safety compliance by enabling real-time monitoring and proactive risk management. According to a 2021 study published in the Journal of Safety Research, organizations that adopted wearable devices saw a remarkable 30% reduction in workplace accidents within the first year of implementation . Companies like Bosch have successfully integrated wearable sensors that track workers' heart rates and fatigue levels, alerting supervisors when an employee may be at risk of injury. This level of insight allows for immediate corrective actions, ultimately fostering a culture of safety that is not only reactive but also preventative.

Furthermore, a case study from construction giant Skanska highlights how smart helmets equipped with Augmented Reality and IoT technologies significantly enhanced safety compliance during their projects. With an impressive 40% increase in safety adherence rates cited in a recent survey, these advanced devices provide workers with real-time data on potential hazards, site changes, and compliance requirements . As wearables continue to evolve, they will remain a cornerstone for companies looking to synchronize technology and safety, effectively bridging the gap between data and action on the ground.

## 6. Data Analytics in Safety Management: How to Leverage Insights for Better Decision-Making

Data analytics plays a pivotal role in enhancing safety management systems by providing insights that drive better decision-making. Leading organizations, such as Amazon and General Electric, have successfully harnessed data analytics to predict and mitigate safety risks. For instance, Amazon utilizes machine learning algorithms to analyze vast datasets from employee interactions and operational processes, allowing them to identify potential hazards and implement preventative measures proactively. GE has incorporated predictive analytics into their safety management framework to monitor equipment performance and reduce incidents related to machinery failure. Research indicates that companies leveraging data analytics can achieve up to a 30% reduction in workplace accidents, highlighting the transformative impact of these technologies on safety outcomes .

To implement data analytics effectively, organizations should focus on cultivating a data-driven culture and investing in training for employees on analytical tools. Companies can apply various approaches, such as real-time monitoring systems powered by Internet of Things (IoT) devices, which continuously gather safety-related data. A notable example is the use of wearables by companies like BP, which track vital signs and environmental conditions of workers in hazardous areas. By analyzing the collected data, BP has improved their safety protocols and reduced incident rates significantly. Furthermore, a report from the National Safety Council emphasizes that organizations that prioritize data analytics not only enhance compliance but also foster a proactive safety culture .

![Vorecol, human resources management system](https://img.vorecol.com/barras_free/en/environment/barra-vorecol-work-environment-en-6.jpg)

## 7. Building a Culture of Safety: Integrating Emerging Technologies into Daily Operations

In the realm of workplace safety, the integration of emerging technologies is not simply a trend; it’s a necessity for building a robust culture of safety. A notable example can be found in the manufacturing giant Siemens, which harnessed the power of augmented reality (AR) and the Internet of Things (IoT) to enhance safety protocols in their factories. By implementing wearable devices equipped with IoT sensors, they reported a remarkable 50% reduction in accidents and near-misses within the first six months (Siemens, 2022). Employees were able to receive real-time alerts about potential hazards, transforming traditional safety training into a dynamic, engaging experience. This paradigm shift signifies how companies can utilize innovative technologies to foster a proactive approach to workplace safety, ultimately leading to a safer environment for their workforce.

Moreover, a study by the National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health (NIOSH) revealed that organizations integrating machine learning algorithms with their safety management systems saw a 40% improvement in identifying risk factors before accidents occurred. Leading organizations like Amazon have adopted predictive analytics not only to streamline their operations but also to identify potential safety issues before they escalate. By analyzing vast amounts of operational data, these companies have created a safety culture where employees are empowered to prioritize their wellbeing actively (NIOSH, 2023). The proactive harnessing of technology in safety measures, combined with continuous education and engagement, will pave the way for a transformational approach that ensures the safety of every worker on the job. For more insights, explore the detailed findings here: [NIOSH Report].

## Final Conclusions

In conclusion, the integration of emerging technologies such as IoT devices, AI-driven analytics, and wearable safety equipment is fundamentally transforming workplace safety management systems. As demonstrated by companies like Siemens, which implemented IoT-enabled monitoring systems to reduce incident rates by 35% (Siemens, 2022), and BP's use of AI to predict hazardous conditions, the potential for these technologies to enhance safety is immense. By adopting comprehensive solutions that integrate real-time data collection, machine learning, and predictive analytics, organizations can proactively address safety risks and foster a culture of safety. For a deeper understanding of the impact of technology in workplace safety, refer to the research conducted by the National Safety Council, which highlights how technological innovations are reshaping safety protocols (NSC, 2021).

To effectively integrate these technologies into operations, companies must prioritize training, stakeholder engagement, and the establishment of clear protocols. A case study on the successful implementation of wearable technology by Honeywell showcases how vital it is to align technology with employee needs. Their smart helmets improved communication on job sites, leading to reduced injuries by 20% (Honeywell, 2023). Organizations should also benchmark against industry leaders and invest in continuous evaluation of their safety management systems. Research by McKinsey emphasizes the importance of leveraging technology not just as a tool but as a catalyst for cultural change within organizations (McKinsey, 2022). With the right approach, companies can ensure that their safety management systems are not only compliant but are also forward-thinking, ultimately leading to safer work environments. For more information, see the following links: [NSC Research], [Siemens Case Study], [Honeywell Smart Helmets].

# What emerging technologies are shaping the future of safety audits?

![sbn-mobile-web-compliance-blog-post](https://sbnsoftware.com/wp-content/uploads/sbn-mobile-web-compliance-blog-post.svg)

In an era defined by rapid technological advancement, the landscape of safety audits is undergoing a transformative shift driven by emerging technologies. As organizations increasingly prioritize safety and compliance, the need for innovative solutions to enhance audit processes has never been more critical. Traditional safety auditing methods, often reliant on paper-based documentation and manual assessments, are being supplanted by cutting-edge tools that streamline data collection, analysis, and reporting. This transformation not only improves efficiency but also enhances the overall accuracy and effectiveness of safety audits.

Emerging technologies such as artificial intelligence (AI), machine learning, the Internet of Things (IoT), and cloud computing are at the forefront of this evolution. AI and machine learning algorithms can analyze vast amounts of safety-related data, identify trends, and predict potential risks with remarkable precision. IoT devices, equipped with sensors and real-time monitoring capabilities, facilitate continuous data collection, allowing organizations to maintain an ongoing overview of safety performance. Meanwhile, cloud-based solutions enhance collaboration and provide access to critical information anytime and anywhere, breaking down barriers associated with traditional audit frameworks.

Moreover, augmented reality (AR) and virtual reality (VR) have begun to play a role in safety training and audits, offering immersive experiences that improve understanding and retention of safety protocols. This integration of technology not only fosters a culture of safety within organizations but also empowers auditors to conduct thorough and insightful evaluations. As the regulatory landscape becomes increasingly complex and the stakes higher, harnessing these technologies is essential for organizations aiming to achieve compliance, reduce risks, and protect their workforce.

In this article, we will explore the various emerging technologies that are shaping the future of safety audits. We will delve into how each technology contributes to the audit process, the benefits they provide, and the challenges organizations may face in their implementation. By understanding these advancements, stakeholders can better prepare for the future of safety auditing and cultivate a proactive approach to risk management within their operations.

![Simple But Needed blog banner image](https://sbnsoftware.com/wp-content/uploads/SBN-Banner.png)
![Simple But Needed blog banner image](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns=%22http://www.w3.org/2000/svg%22%20viewBox=%220%200%20210%20140%22%3E%3C/svg%3E)

## Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning in Safety Audits

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) are revolutionizing the process of safety audits in various industries by enabling more efficient data analysis, risk assessment, and predictive analytics. Traditional safety audit methods often rely heavily on manual processes, which can be time-consuming, error-prone, and sometimes overlook critical safety indicators. In contrast, AI and ML algorithms can analyze vast amounts of data from multiple sources in real time, identifying patterns and anomalies that humans might miss. This capability not only speeds up the audit process but also improves the overall accuracy and comprehensiveness of the assessments.

One of the key advantages of integrating AI and ML into safety audits is their ability to learn from historical data. By examining past incidents, these technologies can develop models that predict potential future safety risks, allowing organizations to take proactive measures before hazards escalate into serious issues. For instance, AI applications can assess worker behaviors, equipment conditions, and environmental factors, providing insights into potential risk areas. This predictive capacity not only helps in mitigating risks but also in minimizing downtime and reducing costs associated with workplace incidents.

Moreover, the integration of AI and ML can facilitate continuous improvement in safety practices. Organizations can utilize real-time data and AI-driven analytics to develop effective safety training programs tailored to their specific operational challenges. By continuously refining safety protocols based on data-driven insights, companies can foster a safer working environment while also enhancing their compliance with industry standards and regulations.

Emerging technologies are further shaping the future of safety audits beyond AI and ML. For instance, the Internet of Things (IoT) enables real-time monitoring of equipment and environmental conditions, providing auditors with valuable data on safety performance. Additionally, blockchain technology is being explored for ensuring the integrity and transparency of safety audit data, which is crucial for accountability and compliance. Virtual reality (VR) and augmented reality (AR) are enhancing training simulations, allowing workers to immerse themselves in safe yet realistic environments where they can learn to respond to hazardous situations effectively. Combined with mobile applications and cloud computing, these technologies are transforming safety audits into a dynamic and proactive process, ensuring that organizations can adapt quickly to changing safety landscapes.

## Internet of Things (IoT) and Connected Devices

The Internet of Things (IoT) and connected devices have become pivotal in transforming the landscape of safety audits across various industries. By enabling the continuous monitoring and analysis of environments, equipment, and personnel, IoT technology facilitates a deeper understanding of safety practices and compliance. Connected devices, such as sensors, wearables, and smart machinery, can collect real-time data on a multitude of parameters, including environmental conditions, equipment performance, and individual compliance with safety protocols. This data is transmitted to centralized systems where it can be analyzed to identify potential hazards, assess compliance, and enhance overall safety measures.

One of the most significant advantages of IoT in safety audits is its ability to provide actionable insights drawn from vast amounts of data. Integrated sensor networks can detect anomalies, such as equipment malfunctions or environmental hazards, prompting immediate alerts to safety officers and decision-makers. This real-time feedback loop enables organizations to respond swiftly to potential safety risks, which could prevent accidents and reduce downtime. Moreover, the use of wearables permits monitoring of worker health and safety in real-time, ensuring that adherence to safety guidelines is maintained, particularly in high-risk environments.

Emerging technologies such as artificial intelligence (AI), machine learning, and advanced data analytics are further enhancing the effectiveness of IoT in safety audits. AI algorithms can analyze the vast datasets collected from IoT devices to identify patterns and predict potential safety breaches before they occur. This predictive capability is invaluable in risk management, allowing organizations to implement proactive measures rather than merely reacting to incidents. Additionally, cloud computing facilitates the storage and processing of these large datasets, enabling organizations to scale their safety audit capabilities efficiently.

In summary, the convergence of IoT and connected devices with emerging technologies is significantly shaping the future of safety audits. Organizations that leverage these advancements not only improve their safety compliance and reduce risks but also create a culture of safety that is informed by data-driven insights. As IoT technology continues to evolve, its impact on safety audits is expected to grow, fostering innovative solutions that enhance workplace safety and efficiency.

## Blockchain for Data Integrity and Transparency

Blockchain technology is rapidly emerging as a transformative force within the realm of safety audits. Its decentralized nature allows for a secure and transparent framework in which safety-related data can be recorded, accessed, and verified by multiple stakeholders without the risk of alteration or unauthorized access. This level of data integrity is crucial for industries that must adhere to stringent safety regulations and standards, such as healthcare, construction, and manufacturing. By utilizing blockchain, organizations can create an immutable ledger of safety practices, incidents, and compliance records that can be scrutinized in real-time, ensuring that all safety measures are documented accurately and transparently.

One of the primary benefits of using blockchain technology in safety audits is the enhanced trust it fosters among participants in the audit process. Traditionally, audits may be marred by discrepancies or data tampering, which can lead to non-compliance and even legal liabilities. However, with blockchain, every transaction, or data entry related to safety practices, is transparent and traceable. This not only helps in maintaining compliance with regulatory requirements but also allows organizations to proactively address potential safety issues before they lead to incidents. The ability to authenticate data in real-time means that auditors can ensure compliance checks are based on the most current and accurate information, leading to better decision-making and improved safety outcomes.

Moreover, blockchain technology can facilitate collaboration among various stakeholders involved in safety audits, including regulatory bodies, auditors, and organizations. Smart contracts—automated agreements coded into blockchain—can trigger specific actions based on predetermined conditions. For instance, if safety equipment data logged on the blockchain indicates a failure, it can automatically alert the necessary personnel for immediate action. This not only streamlines the audit process but also reduces the time and resources spent on manual verification and dispute resolution.

In summary, blockchain is revolutionizing the safety audit landscape by promoting data integrity, enhancing transparency, and facilitating collaborative auditing practices. As organizations increasingly recognize the necessity of maintaining rigorous safety standards, the integration of emerging technologies like blockchain will play a pivotal role in shaping the future of safety audits. As industries continue to evolve, the adoption of such technologies will be key in delivering effective and reliable safety inspections, ultimately contributing to a safer work environment.

## Virtual Reality (VR) and Augmented Reality (AR) for Training and Simulation

Virtual Reality (VR) and Augmented Reality (AR) are revolutionizing the way safety audits and training programs are conducted across various industries. By providing immersive and interactive environments, these technologies facilitate experiences that enable employees to learn and practice safety protocols in a risk-free settings. For instance, VR can simulate hazardous scenarios, allowing workers to understand proper safety measures without the actual dangers present in the real world. This type of experiential learning is highly effective, as it engages users more fully than traditional training methods.

Furthermore, AR can enhance on-the-job training by overlaying important safety information and instructions directly onto a worker’s view of their environment. For example, AR glasses can display safety guidelines, machinery operation instructions, or emergency procedures in real-time as employees perform their tasks. This immediate access to relevant information can significantly reduce the cognitive load on workers while helping them adhere to safety protocols. With these technologies, organizations can tailor training modules to the specific environments and risks faced by their employees, thereby improving the overall effectiveness of safety audits and compliance.

Emerging technologies are continually shaping the future of safety audits beyond VR and AR. The integration of AI and machine learning algorithms can analyze vast amounts of data collected from safety audits, identifying patterns and trends that human auditors might overlook. IoT devices enhance real-time monitoring of workplace conditions, enabling immediate responses to any safety hazards. Furthermore, the use of blockchain technology can ensure that audit data remains tamper-proof and verifiable, increasing transparency and trust. As organizations increasingly adopt these technologies, the safety audit process becomes more proactive and data-driven, leading to improved safety outcomes and reduced incidents in the workplace.

![Simple But Needed blog banner image](https://sbnsoftware.com/wp-content/uploads/SBN-Banner.png)
![Simple But Needed blog banner image](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns=%22http://www.w3.org/2000/svg%22%20viewBox=%220%200%20210%20140%22%3E%3C/svg%3E)

## Mobile Applications and Cloud Computing for Real-time Reporting

Mobile applications and cloud computing are revolutionizing the landscape of safety audits by facilitating real-time reporting and enhancing collaboration among teams. The integration of these technologies allows safety personnel to access, collect, and report data from almost anywhere, significantly increasing efficiency and responsiveness. With user-friendly mobile apps, inspectors can conduct audits directly on-site, using smartphones or tablets to capture observations, take photographs, and document findings immediately. This immediacy helps to eliminate the lag in reporting that often complicates traditional audit processes, ensuring that corrective actions can be taken promptly.

Cloud computing plays a crucial role in this ecosystem by enabling the storage and management of vast amounts of data in a centralized location. Cloud platforms provide easy access to audit data, allowing team members to collaborate effectively. Safety managers can analyze audit trends over time, comparing historical data to identify persistent issues or improvements. Moreover, the cloud also facilitates better communication between different departments and external stakeholders, as data can be shared seamlessly and securely, breaking down silos that often hinder safety initiatives.

The impact of these technologies extends beyond mere convenience. By leveraging mobile applications and cloud computing, organizations can enhance compliance with safety regulations and standards. Real-time reporting enables companies to maintain an up-to-date overview of their safety practices, allowing them to identify non-conformities quickly and take corrective actions before problems escalate. Furthermore, the capability of generating real-time dashboards and insights fosters a proactive safety culture within organizations, encouraging continuous improvement and accountability among employees. As these technologies advance, the future of safety audits will likely see an even greater integration of mobile applications and cloud solutions, leading to heightened safety standards and enhanced operational efficiency.

# News and Articles

## News

#### Share

# Emerging Technologies in Safety: Adoption Trends & Effects on Workplace Safety Outcomes

#### [Sponsored by Grainger](https://www.grainger.com/content/mc/ehs?gucid=N:N:SC:Owned:WWG:CSM-7745:R4FHR5:20240101:APZ_1)

Grainger experts will discuss emerging safety technologies, sharing new insights into what organizations are using and how, and reviewing how these technologies affect injury, fatality, productivity and efficiency outcomes. Topics include safety management and learning management systems, as well as what is on the horizon with artificial intelligence, Internet of Things, and virtual and augmented reality training. We will also explain which industries are planning to implement these new technologies over the next year and what the technology landscape will look like in the near future.

**What You Will Learn**

### Speakers

**Matt Law, DrPH, CSP, REHS**  
**Manager, Customer Safety Strategy, W. W. Grainger Inc.**  
![](/images/default-source/default-album/matt-law.jpg?sfvrsn=d9d17846_1)Matt Law is an avid researcher of emerging technologies and trends in EHS and business, and he develops comprehensive solutions to mitigate risk to people, property and the environment. He holds an M.P.H. from West Virginia University and is a U.S. Navy Reserve environmental health officer. Law is the PR/Sponsorships Committee Chair for ASSP’s Central Florida Chapter and serves on the Board of Directors for the Society for Total Worker Health. He is a member of ASSP’s Emerging Professionals in OSH Common Interest Group and its Consultants and Training and Communications practice specialties. He has also been published in Professional Safety.

![](/images/default-source/default-album/matt-law.jpg?sfvrsn=d9d17846_1)

**Rick Cowman, QSSP**  
**Senior Manager, Digital Environmental Health and Safety (EH&S) Solutions, W.W. Grainger, Inc.**  
![](/images/default-source/default-album/rick-cowman.jpg?sfvrsn=d8d17846_1)Rick Cowman has nearly 25 years of technology experience in the safety industry. He was founder and president of Safety Certified Inc., an early developer of SaaS solutions to help customers track and manage all aspects of safety and ensure regulatory compliance. Grainger acquired the company in 2010, hiring Cowman to lead the development of Grainger Digital EH&S Services. He has been instrumental in forming strategic partnerships with leading technology companies in areas such as learning management, incident tracking, inspection and asset management, as well as emerging AI and IoT technologies such as machine/computer vision, virtual and augmented reality training, and the connected worker.

![](/images/default-source/default-album/rick-cowman.jpg?sfvrsn=d8d17846_1)

--- SOURCE 4: New safety technology in the workplace ---
URL: https://www.tdi.texas.gov/tips/safety/safety-technology.html

SUMMARY:
1 Drones. · 2 Work zone intrusion detection. · 3 Sensor technology. · 4 Proximity devices. · 5 Lone worker monitoring. · 6 Digital gas monitors. · 7 Computer vision

FULL CONTENT:
![Texas Department of Insurance](../../_global/images/tdi-logo-new-web.png)

Â

# New safety technology in the workplace

![New safety technology in the workplace - man in hard hat in front of a screen](../artwork/safetytech.png)

![New safety technology in the workplace - man in hard hat in front of a screen](../artwork/safetytech.png)

The future of workplace safety is here, transforming how companies protect employees on the job. From flying robots that inspect dangerous areas to smart sensors that warn of invisible threats, modern safety technology is not just responding to accidents – it is preventing them before they happen. Here are some examples of high-tech tools making workplaces more productive and safer:

## 1 **Drones.**

These small, remote-controlled flying devices with cameras and sensors can inspect large or dangerous areas from a safe distance, spot hazards, and alert workers to moving equipment without putting anyone at risk.

## 2 **Work zone intrusion detection.**

These systems alert workers when unauthorized people or vehicles enter restricted areas. They prevent accidents caused by unexpected entry and increase overall awareness of the surroundings.

## 3 **Sensor technology****.**

Smal sensory devices can detect various hazards in the workplace. Air quality sensors warn of harmful gases or pollutants. Temperature sensors prevent overheating in industrial settings. Motion sensors alert workers to moving vehicles and machinery.

## 4 **Proximity devices****.**

These sensors detect when objects or people are too close to dangerous equipment. They can automatically stop or slow machinery when workers get near, reducing the risk of crush injuries or accidents with moving parts.

## 5 **Lone worker monitoring****.**

This technology tracks employees working alone and provides regular check-ins to ensure worker safety. It also gives lone workers a way to call for help if needed and ensures a quick response in case of emergencies.

## 6 **Digital gas monitors****.**

These portable devices detect and measure harmful gases in the air. They alert workers to dangerous gas levels before they become life-threatening and provide real-time data on air quality in work areas. This can help prevent gas-related accidents and long-term health issues.

## 7 **Computer vision and camera analytics with artificial intelligence (AI)****.**

These advanced camera systems use AI to analyze video feeds and automatically detect unsafe behavior or conditions. They provide 24/7 monitoring of work areas and can identify potential hazards that human observers might miss.

The National Safety Council (NSC) has identified more than 100 relevant health and safety technologies that can significantly reduce the risk of accidents, protect workers from harm, and create a safer, more secure workplace for everyone. For more information on current and emerging safety technology, visit NSC's [WORKtoZERO](https://www.nsc.org/workplace/safety-topics/work-to-zero/safety-technologies "National Safety Council  - Work to Zero") webpage. For details on many other workplace safety topics, download or stream DWC’s free [publications](https://www.tdi.texas.gov/wc/safety/videoresources/index.html#A "safety publications") and [videos](https://wwwapps.tdi.texas.gov/inter/perlroot/sasweb9/cgi-bin/broker.exe?_service=wcExt&_program=progExt.safety_videos.sas "safety videos"), or contact a DWC Workplace Safety training instructor at 800-242-7031, option 2, or [SafetyTraining@tdi.texas.gov](mailto:SafetyTraining@tdi.texas.gov "email Workplace Safety staff").

Â

--- SOURCE 5: The Future of Workplace Safety Technology Is Now - Part One - NCCI ---
URL: https://www.ncci.com/Articles/Pages/Insights-Future-Workplace-Safety-Technology-Now.aspx

SUMMARY:
AI/Computer Vision—Computer vision and AI systems use cameras to help detect workers' potentially unsafe movements and hazards in the work

FULL CONTENT:
![](/_layouts/15/images/spcommon.png?rev=44)
![](/PublishingImages/NCCILogo-Teal.png)
![Search](/_layouts/15/images/searchresultui.png?rev=44#ThemeKey=searchresultui)
![](/PublishingImages/Navigation-Image-ProductCatalog.jpg)
![](/PublishingImages/Navigation-Image-CareerSite.jpg)
![](/PublishingImages/Navigation-Image-NCCIAcademy.jpg)
![](/PublishingImages/Navigation-Image-LearningCenter-2.jpg)
![](/PublishingImages/MenuImg1.png)
![Search](/_layouts/15/images/searchresultui.png?rev=44#ThemeKey=searchresultui)
![](/PublishingImages/Bullet.gif)
![](/PublishingImages/Thumbnail[ASE][DamianEngland].jpg)
![](/PublishingImages/Insights-YuchenS-Thumbnail.jpg)
![](/PublishingImages/Insights-RajiC-Thumbnail.jpg)
![](/PublishingImages/Thumbnail[RSE][LauraKersey].jpg)
![](/PublishingImages/Thumbnail-Future-Workplace.jpg)

*There’s a growing buzz in workers compensation that technology, the workplace, and the role of workers are changing more dramatically today and at a faster pace than ever before. Along with shifting jobs and evolving workplaces come new and changing exposures to worker injuries. Questions continue to arise about the status and evolution of safety technologies. In fact, some insurers are testing or discussing these technologies, and in some cases, providing them to their customers/policyholders. Although the application of advanced technology for safety purposes is not new, as evident in the use of telematics in the automobile industry for many years, it is still fairly new and evolving in more traditional workplaces.*

*Based on interviews with multiple workers compensation insurers, safety technology vendors/suppliers, and insureds, this series is a presentation of perspectives from various stakeholders. In this article, the first installment of NCCI’s series “The Future of Workplace Safety Technology Is Now,” we explore carrier viewpoints on the latest trends in safety technology.*

## Key Insights

## Introduction

The evolving workplace and role of the worker are top of mind for workers compensation stakeholders. A rising industry-wide concern about the shifting workforce and workplace is the potential impact on the frequency and severity of on-the-job injuries. As part of our ongoing dialogue with workers compensation stakeholders around the country, NCCI is often asked about the status of safety technology being utilized in the workplace, such as wearable devices.

In this first article of our three-part series, we relay the perspectives gained from separately interviewed representatives from four insurance companies in various stages of testing, introducing, and implementing safety technology.

## Carrier Interview Questions and Answers

### Here are some of the key questions from these interviews:

The safety technology industry has evolved since NCCI published its first article on this topic.1 In 2019, NCCI interviewed three insurers that were exploring the use of wearables in the workplace. We followed up with one of those insurers for this article and interviewed three additional workers compensation insurers. The four insurers that we interviewed for this article are currently using or exploring multiple types of safety technologies, including wearables, Artificial Intelligence (AI)/Computer Vision, the Internet of Things (IoT), software applications, and drones.

![](/PublishingImages/Safety-Tech-Timeline.jpg)

### What are these safety technologies and what do they do?

![](/PublishingImages/ICONS-Wearables.jpg)

![](/PublishingImages/ICONS-Wearables.jpg)

**Wearables**—A wearable may include sensors that are worn on the body. Industrial wearables are generally classified into four categories by function:2

![](/PublishingImages/ICONS-ComputerVision.jpg)

![](/PublishingImages/ICONS-ComputerVision.jpg)

**AI/Computer Vision**—Computer vision and AI systems use cameras to help detect workers’ potentially unsafe movements and hazards in the work environment and provide real-time warnings. An employer can use these technologies to evaluate, assess, and take actions that aid prevention of worker injuries.

![](/PublishingImages/ICONS-IoT.jpg)

![](/PublishingImages/ICONS-IoT.jpg)

**Internet of Things (IoT)**—IoT sensors are pieces of hardware that can monitor changes in an environment, including temperature, pressure, and motion, and collect data about those changes. If these sensors are connected to a network, they can share data with that network. For example, a smart system can adjust the lighting, temperature, etc., in the workplace, as necessary.

![](/PublishingImages/ICONS-Software.jpg)

![](/PublishingImages/ICONS-Software.jpg)

**Software Applications**—Software applications can work together with video recordings or other tools for a more complete risk assessment. For example, an employer can use a mobile phone to record a video of a worker performing various tasks and then upload the video to a software application to assess risk and hazards and suggest improvements.

![](/PublishingImages/ICONS-Drones.jpg)

![](/PublishingImages/ICONS-Drones.jpg)

**Drones**—Unmanned Aircraft Systems (UAS) can evaluate certain exposures without putting workers at risk for injury. For example, drones can evaluate roofing conditions and cell phone towers, as well as monitor air quality in confined spaces.

These safety technologies can work together to provide real-time workplace monitoring and possibly help prevent injuries. For example, if a worker is in a hazardous space, a
**wearable** can track the worker’s location and
**AI/Computer Vision** can monitor the environment and provide real-time warnings.

### How can safety solutions add value?

There is a wide variation among the many safety technologies currently in use or in testing. The insurers we interviewed provided the following examples of how some of the various technologies are being used in practice:

These safety technologies gather data about workers and the workplace, which can be used for real-time or hindsight analysis to improve worker safety. The interviewees reported that analysis of the data and the resulting safety enhancements are critical to the successful implementation of safety technology.

It was noted that the volume of data is vast, and methods for its compilation and analysis are still evolving. Currently, the carriers we interviewed said they rely primarily on results and tools provided by the safety technology vendors and they are still in an early stage of analyzing the data to quantify any impact on claim frequencies and severities. While the return on investment from utilizing safety technology can be measured through data analysis, other impacts—such as improvement in worker safety and reduced disruption of operations—are harder to quantify but may be of even greater value.

### Are there specific injury types or industries that may benefit more from newer safety technologies?

Back injuries, including sprains and strains, are a primary injury area of focus for safety technologies, according to the workers compensation insurers interviewed. Back injuries are especially prevalent in the
**manufacturing**,
**logistics**, and
**warehousing** industries and can be costly.

According to NCCI data,3 lower-back injuries rank first in lost-time claim counts (roughly 12%), third in claim dollars (roughly 11%), and “middle of the pack” in average severity, which is about $40,000 per claim. The top three body part injuries that involve lost-time claims are lower-back, knee(s), and shoulder(s).

![](/PublishingImages/CommonlyInjuredBodyParts.jpg)

Since back injuries are relatively common in the
**manufacturing**,
**logistics**, and
**warehousing** industries, it is no surprise that the insurers we spoke with identified these industries as most likely to benefit from advanced safety technology. The insurers noted that, while the current safety technologies are more prevalent in traditional four-walled environments, such as warehouses, advancements in safety technologies are expanding to other workspaces.

### What are the obstacles to implementation?

The insurers interviewed indicated they had
**mixed results** from implementing new safety technologies. They noted several obstacles to implementation, including privacy concerns, cost and return on investment (ROI), company culture, and change management.

### What is the future of safety technology? Is it a game-changer?

According to all insurers interviewed, advanced safety technology will be part of the future of workers compensation. However, one key to success will be the ability to analyze and fully understand data from these technologies. Addressing and overcoming barriers to implementation is another challenge.

When asked if safety technology is a “game-changer,” the responses varied, ranging from “It can be …” to “Absolutely.” Safety technology was mentioned as a potential differentiator to offer higher service and value. It was also noted that “safety technology will point out problems but may not point out solutions. But pinpointing the problem could lead to a solution.”

Whether safety technology will become a game-changer for workers compensation remains to be seen. From a broader perspective, working toward the common goal of preventing job-related injuries is good for all workers compensation stakeholders. As the workers compensation industry focuses on underwriting risks, claims costs, and improved outcomes for injured workers, it is natural for stakeholders to explore new ways to prevent or reduce worker injuries.

## Conclusion

Workplace injury frequency has been decreasing for decades, reflecting improvements in ergonomics, safety rules and laws, technological advancements such as automation and robotics, and overall increased safety awareness and education by insurers and employers. During our interviews, we heard exciting comments about the growth of technologies powered by artificial intelligence and increased computing power. But none of these would make for a safe workplace without employers adopting a culture of safety and embracing the benefit of a safe workplace for employers and employees alike.

Loss prevention programs empowered by innovative technology may enable further gains in workplace safety for employers while also providing value for insurers. One unanimous message we heard during our interviews is that insurers believe advanced technology holds promise for the future of workers compensation.

## Up Next

*The second article in our series will feature insights from safety technology vendors/suppliers.*

*The articles in this series reflect the interviewees’ opinions on the topic. Special thanks to the insurers that generously shared their thoughts: AF Group​ (including Accident Fund, United Heartland, CompWest, and Third Coast Underwriters), EMC Insurance Companies, Erie Insurance Group, and Nationwide Mutual Insurance Company.*

1Laura Kersey, “[Technology at Work: Wearables in Workers Compensation](/Articles/Pages/II_Insights_Wearables.aspx),”
**ncci.com**, June 24, 2019, accessed on August 4, 2023.  
2Ekaterina Svertoka et al., “[Wearables for Industrial Work Safety: A Survey](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC8199604/#B18-sensors-21-03844),” NIH National Library of Medicine, June 2, 2021, accessed on August 4, 2023.  
3NCCI's Unit Statistical Data, Accident Years 2016–2021.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Innovative tools like AI, IoT, and VR are reshaping safety analytics, enhancing real-time monitoring, predictive analytics, and risk assessments to prevent workplace incidents effectively.
Conducting extended research over time can provide insights into the effectiveness of safety technologies, allowing organizations to refine practices and enhance workplace safety standards.
Analyzing safety practices across different sectors can reveal best practices and innovative solutions, fostering a collaborative approach to enhance safety measures universally.
